title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure-related items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines of $100 per day. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road and water/sewer connections on Radkey Avenue.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Review 2025 Weston Water Utility PSC annual report
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkey Avenue duplex

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. The project will likely move from 2029 to 2028. Staff noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past four to five years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue road repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for settlement issues over sanitary sewer trenches, with understanding that the contractor will return if problems recur.

Staff reported road limits were lifted April 29th. Crews performed significant dewatering near Weston Avenue tributary for about a week due to heavy rain and runoff. Discussion addressed ongoing flooding issues in the Crane Meadows area, with staff looking at permanent pump solutions. The Kennedy Park Farmers Market received positive feedback with no parking or traffic complaints reported.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration into the sanitary system, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into the water system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. Staff identified sump pump discharges as a major contributor and noted an upcoming INI study with In-Pipe Technologies to identify problem areas. Well 2 rehab at Carrie started the day of the meeting.

discussions.item: 2025 Weston Water Utility PSC Annual Report
discussions.body: Michael presented the annual report showing operating revenues of approximately $3 million and net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. Water loss was reported at 6%. The committee moved to acknowledge the report, which passed unanimously.

The resolution drafted by the League of Wisconsin Municipalities supports long-term sustainable funding solutions for state transportation. Michael noted the village has decreased its public works budget in recent years and emphasized that state funding doesn't cover what the public thinks it does, requiring subsidies from general tax dollars. The committee recommended village board adoption, passed unanimously.

King Gravel submitted the low quote at $4.23 per ton for crushing approximately 30,000 tons of recycled material at the Ryan Street site, totaling $126,900. Michael calculated the village saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to the City of Wausau's costs, representing about $130,000 in savings across the three 2026 projects. The committee awarded the contract to King Gravel unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement fines for residents illegally discharging sump pumps and other clear water into the sanitary system. The committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. After discussion, the committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines of $100 per day, passed unanimously. Staff will also calculate the dollar impact of the excess clear water treatment.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: A duplex builder requested temporary water and sewer connection for lot 1 on Radkey Avenue via long lateral service until the street is reconstructed. The committee added language specifying Radkey Avenue reconstruction would occur no later than 2029, at which time the property owner would pay assessment for main extension. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helen Street. Weston's share for water and sanitary sewer utilities is estimated at $94,860. Steam Construction will hold the contract through Rothschild. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Construction Service Proposals - Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Projects
discussions.body: Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For the Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA was selected at $37,315 (lower than Clark Dietz at $48,910). For Bladel Avenue, Clark Dietz was recommended at $73,730 despite being slightly higher than MSA's $70,400, due to having full-time experienced staff rather than a summer intern. Motion passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton, estimated $126,900 total
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day fines
Authorize water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for estimated $94,860 in utility costs
Approve MSA for Alderson/Jelnik construction services and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue construction services
Schedule special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue on Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of excess clear water treatment for next meeting
Staff to provide street lighting update for future meeting

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission also heard that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from April 21st meeting
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: The commission elected Trustee Erling as Chair, Cushing as Vice Chair (seconded by Jackson), and Renee as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously with no opposition.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) presented the grant request, reporting estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members noted the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year and requested better tracking of hotel room usage from Holiday Inn Express. The commission approved a $5,000 grant, matching last year's amount, with a request for improved attendance and room data in the post-event report. Motion passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, describing how the candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese. She noted this was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s, and the candidates were thankful for the community's hospitality.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved site plans for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. The commission also scheduled the first steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan update for June 1st.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Future agenda items and staff updates

discussions.item: Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Chief Jeremy explained the building orientation was changed to face east due to a gas pipeline easement, which reduced fill costs. The building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond the current 40 officers. Discussion included an alternate storage shed location, landscaping compliance, and the gas pipeline under the parking lot (approved by ANR/gas company). A motion was made to recommend approval to the board of trustees subject to staff conditions 2-5. The motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff presented a proposed inspection program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection for structural integrity and a winter operational inspection for material handling practices. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and should be contacted. The commission approved the program with an amendment to report back to both the Plan Commission and Public Works Committee. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: The commission approved the staff report without discussion. Motion passed unanimously.
The commission approved the building permit report without discussion. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly update
discussions.body: Staff announced the first steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m., with a joint session of the Plan Commission and Village Board. Staff will send calendar invites.

Electric vehicle charging stations were noted as a future agenda item, likely coming in the fall. A commissioner asked about potential TIF districts for residential/workforce housing. Staff mentioned Ehlers has put together a presentation on residential TIF qualifications that could be shared with the commission or incorporated into the comprehensive plan steering committee discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via video call. He asked the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project to avoid excessive taxpayer costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and findings.
actionItems: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan recommended for approval to Board of Trustees subject to staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program approved for implementation with reporting to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda
Staff to share Ehlers presentation on residential TIF district qualifications with commission

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from department heads on their missions, budgets, and operations. No action items were voted on except approval of April 1st meeting minutes, which passed unanimously.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute (no items)
Review of standing committee ordinances, missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Department presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes. Vice Chair Rosenberg seconded. The motion passed unanimously with all in favor.

discussions.item: Review of standing committee ordinances and structure
discussions.body: Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction over the Health Department, Social Services, and Veteran Services, plus affiliated entities North Central Health Care and the Aging Disability Resource Center. He outlined reporting relationships with statutory boards including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the committee will focus on developing a new strategic plan by December 2025.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's mission of preventing disease and protecting public health. She highlighted 1,200 licenses overseen for restaurants, pools, and hotels; communicable disease prevention including recent tick awareness efforts; the Nurse Family Partnership program for pregnant mothers; and a new FTE position for homeless system response created following a county task force. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE staff. Administrator Leonard noted the department's $5 million budget is roughly half tax levy, with significant intergovernmental grants and public charges for environmental health services.

Administrator Leonard provided an overview in the director's absence, explaining the department's divisions: child welfare (child protective services and youth justice), children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. With nearly 140 FTE, it's the county's second-largest department. He highlighted the successful Family Keys pilot program addressing housing instability for child welfare and youth justice cases, noting the program substantively changed based on board feedback and has helped families who never had credit accounts or formal leases.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported his office grew to 3.0 FTE in 2025 and saw customer interactions increase from 5,900 to 8,149. He noted $44 million in annual VA compensation flows to Marathon County veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo highlighted housing stability efforts through partnership with Center for Veterans Issues and suicide prevention initiatives, noting 7 of Marathon County's 30 suicides in 2025 were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional cooperation; Galindo explained state statute requires each county have a VSO but veterans can work with any office. He acknowledged staffing challenges, noting he was booking 6 weeks out when short-staffed.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) as a one-stop shop for aging and disability services. Programs include information and assistance, caregiver support, dementia care, falls prevention, benefit specialists, and Meals on Wheels nutrition services. He reported significant financial challenges with federal funding flat since 2024 while nutrition costs increased 40-45% over four years, forcing reduced meals, service days, and staffing. Marathon County's levy contribution is $413,135. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate meals program at the Hmong American Center.

actionItems: Approved April 1st committee meeting minutes unanimously
North Central Health Care to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full county board
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion items to upcoming meetings
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as initially stated)

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed ordinances regulating mobile food vendors and e-bikes, and discussed ongoing concerns about the farmers market location at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and approved multiple license renewals for the 2026-2027 term.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees amendment
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, and e-bikes
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fees Amendment
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment criticizing the lack of detailed operational data justifying the fees. Trustee Motti expressed support, noting the justified need as population increases. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional inspection system where food trucks inspected by one participating municipality (including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others) receive a sticker recognized by all participating communities, eliminating duplicate inspections. Ordinance 26-008 was approved with motion by Lope, second by Zagami, passing unanimously.

The board discussed Ordinance 26-012 amending traffic regulations for bicycles, with trustees clarifying the difference between e-bikes and e-motos (which can reach 52 mph). The ordinance was approved with motion by Zagami, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote attendance policy. Trustees Beck and others pushed to remove language requiring specific health/safety/welfare justifications, making the policy broader as discussed at the previous meeting. An amendment was made to strike sentences requiring specific reasons for remote attendance, with only notification, camera use, and closed session exclusion requirements remaining. The amendment passed 5-2 with Hermling and Motti voting no. The original motion as amended also passed 5-2 with the same dissenting votes.

The board approved a secondhand article dealer and jewelry dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief Hunt explained the license requirements for businesses buying/selling precious metals and the safety measures for tracking potentially stolen merchandise. The motion was amended to require satisfactory completion of background check and receipt of occupancy permit, as the location is still under construction. Approved unanimously as amended.

The board discussed ongoing concerns about the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park after losing the Camp Phillips/Scoffield Avenue site to development. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about parking on grass along Alderson Street. Trustee Motti noted Mount Miller Park was considered but deemed too far from traffic. Renee explained the market has grown from 25 to 53 vendor spots and confirmed the market operates at no financial loss due to grants and vendor fees. No action was taken; this was discussion only.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, approved unanimously. The board agreed to include applicant resumes in future packets.

During public works report, Michael explained the difficulty filling the engineering technician position due to a shortage of qualified candidates in central Wisconsin, noting even consulting firms are pulling staff from southeast Wisconsin. This vacancy is driving the need for outside engineering consultants.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke twice. During general public comment, he criticized the $108,000 cost for outside engineering consultants, questioned why the engineering technician position remains unfilled, complained that Lisa Beck was introduced as a SAFER board member before official appointment, and criticized the lack of detailed Kennedy Park information in the packet. During the park impact fee hearing, he criticized the lack of operational data justifying the fee increase, stating the village is 'flying by the seat of our pants.'
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased effective upon ordinance passage
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1 at 6:00 PM as joint meeting with plan commission
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement with Town of Ringle for building inspection assistance
Speed study on Weston Avenue to be completed and brought back to board for speed limit determination
Engineering technician position continues to be advertised
Staff to bring applicant resumes to board in future packets for committee appointments
Remote meeting attendance policy updated with broader language
Tourism committee to discuss potential increase in aquatic center funding beyond current $40,000

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected two at-large executive committee members (Supervisors Hapa and Arstead), and unanimously approved a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather and tornado damage in Ringle and other communities. The board suspended its rules to fast-track the tipping fee resolution in response to the ongoing emergency cleanup.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments presentation and remarks from Chair
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution D15 - Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution D16 - Social services budget amendment
Resolution E17 - Modification to landfill tipping fees

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, noting that over 34% of the board is in their first or second term and emphasizing the need for mentorship and leadership development. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but not unanimous. Both county board chair and administrator appointments were included in the single motion.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in favor of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's youngest and oldest residents. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The consent agenda was approved unanimously by voice vote.

discussions.item: Resolution D15 - Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution (24-26) was carried but not unanimous with no discussion.

discussions.item: Resolution D16 - Social Services Budget Amendment
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. Resolution 25-26 was carried but not unanimous with no discussion.

discussions.item: Resolution E17 - Landfill Tipping Fee Modification
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented the emergency tipping fee reduction following severe weather that caused significant damage in Ringle and other communities. The state waived $13.10 per ton, Ringle waived $2.33, and the county proposed reducing fees to approximately $36.94 per ton to cover costs. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special dispensation to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely for affected residents. Supervisor Rosenberg asked for continued discussion. The board voted unanimously to suspend rules to bring the resolution forward, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously on motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested the county waive fees entirely for six months, noting that the state, DNR, and Ringle were all waiving their portions and that private businesses had donated significant time and resources to cleanup. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, noting that many insurance adjusters haven't yet arrived and requesting more time and fee relief for underinsured residents.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 24-26 approving out of home placement reserve increase approved
Resolution 25-26 amending social services budget approved
Resolution 26-26 modifying landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris approved
Administrator to work with Community Foundation on mechanism to reimburse unreimbursed insurance costs for landowners
Solid waste management board to develop clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Staff to track certified disaster-related waste and apply reduced tipping fees through June 17 state emergency declaration period

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the full county board. The fee waiver, originally proposed to help properties impacted by the April 17, 2026 tornado, was amended to cover only septic system reconnect fees rather than all zoning and pouts fees.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy/recreation to general agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Introduction and background of departments appearing before committee
Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview presentation
Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview presentation

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made the motion to approve, with Supervisor Drebeck providing the second. The motion passed unanimously with no further discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval. Supervisor Kurt seconded. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from conservancy/recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Lever asked about the conservancy designation, and Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and the rezoning would make these conforming uses. He confirmed no natural areas would be lost. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve, Supervisor Drew seconded. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and pouts fees for properties impacted by the April 17, 2026 tornado, affecting towns of Ringle, Easton, villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Sefelt asked about potential levy impacts. Supervisor Drebeck proposed an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, excluding other pouts and zoning fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution was then voted on and passed, though not unanimously, to be forwarded to the county board.

Director David Hagenbusher provided an overview of landfill operations, explaining they accept about 1,000 tons daily, have partnerships with Portage and Shawano counties, and began injecting renewable natural gas into pipelines in September 2025. He discussed upcoming capital expenses including an $6 million 11-acre construction project and a required leachate treatment facility by 2032 due to new PFAS regulations. He noted hauling costs for April approached $90,000 due to significant rainfall.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees who serve both county and city of Wausau facilities. He highlighted that Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefelt announced Tom's retirement effective June 1st, with Joe Tucker selected as his replacement. Committee members thanked Lovelin for his nearly 30 years of service.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution (covering only septic reconnect fees) to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Forward Tim Veland rezoning petition approval to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition approval to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Town of Green Valley should update comprehensive plan to reflect zoning change if applicable
Town of McMillan should update comprehensive plan to reflect zoning change if applicable
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to severe weather fee waivers

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved kayak launch signs for installation along the Eau Claire River, deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and Yellow Banks disc golf course maintenance. Public commenters expressed opposition to dogs at the pool and questioned Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Eau Claire River Trail discussion

discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the previous meeting minutes. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

Director Sean Oster provided a timeline document showing Kennedy Park's master plan progress from 2023 to present, including the May 9th ribbon cutting for youth baseball fields. Staff explained the Friends of Kennedy Park are fundraising for field lights, currently at approximately $85,000 toward a $117,000 goal. Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital funds, with staff confirming no village dollars are currently earmarked pending fundraising progress. A trustee defended the Friends group as volunteers who shouldn't be pressured to release financials before their planned public announcement at their June tournament.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Dan provided photos and maps showing tree removal locations along the river and wood mat installation over gas pipelines. The mats were placed to protect TC Energy pipelines during equipment access. Staff showed photos of the large trees that had fallen into the river, noting one would make passage impassible when water levels drop. The village retained some extra mats for future trail projects.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to next month because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. The chair noted her objection from the earlier public commenter would be noted but wanted Katrina present to speak on the item she had requested.

discussions.item: Graffiti on Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches, noting limited equipment access to the trail. The pressure washer on a tandem axle trailer cannot fit on the 8,000-pound bridges. Committee members discussed potential solutions including anti-vandalism coatings, sealers, and volunteer workdays. No formal action was taken, but staff will explore cleaning options and potentially organizing volunteer assistance.

Dan from Marathon County presented version seven of proposed kayak launch signs for three Weston locations (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). Signs will include river maps, QR codes linking to river gauge data, and location-specific history panels. The signs are funded through Marathon County Parks Foundation grants with no village funding required. A motion to approve installation was made, seconded, and passed unanimously.

Staff requested committee interest in discussing food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds after receiving an email from an advocacy group. The committee agreed to add this topic for future discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler the following Tuesday.

publicComment: Two members of the public commented. Andy from 6504 [street inaudible] spoke against allowing dogs in the public pool, stating animals should not be allowed and suggesting dogs belong in rivers and lakes. Jim Pensan (appearing online as 'Vern M') from 5002 [street] Weston criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation, questioned Kennedy Park fundraising finances as 'convoluted and misleading,' and demanded to know where grant money and Glow Games proceeds were being held.
actionItems: Approved installation of kayak launch signs at three Village of Weston locations (Highway J, Ross Avenue, Yellow Banks) - motion passed unanimously
Deferred Dog Day at aquatic center discussion to June meeting
Staff to explore graffiti removal solutions for Eau Claire River Trail benches
Staff to research anti-vandalism coatings and volunteer cleanup opportunities for trail
Food allergy awareness playground signage added as future agenda topic
Mock Miller master plan kickoff meeting scheduled with Rettler for following Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved minutes and acknowledged budget reports before receiving an educational presentation on the Municipal Clerk's office operations, including elections, licensing, and tax collection. Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down and moving away from Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's office budget and operations
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes from the previous Finance and HR meeting was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports
discussions.body: A committee member asked about the timeline for final bond payoff for TIFF districts. Staff explained they anticipate October-November for completion, with bonds due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, likely this summer. Staff confirmed the numbers still look good and they will be able to pay off the debt.
Items five, six, and seven (April budget status report, TIFF detail reports, and legal details report) were acknowledged together. Motion by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented on the clerk's office operations, staffing (two full-time and one part-time employee, down from four full-time plus two assistants previously), and budget. Key topics included election administration with Badger Books (electronic poll books at $11,000 for four units), licensing (51 liquor licenses), tax collection (84% collected at the counter totaling $22 million), and pet licensing brought back in-house saving approximately $5,500 annually. Discussion occurred about pet licensing fee increases from $10 to $20 for neutered dogs due to Marathon County raising fees to cover Humane Society costs. The quarterly newsletter 'The Wire' costs approximately $30,000 annually for printing and postage to 5,089 postal patrons.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well over the past three Saturdays. The market has expanded to include coffee vendors, new food trucks, and craft vendors. Vendors reported being very happy with the setup and location. Committee members raised concerns about parking congestion on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff confirming parking is now allowed on the west side of Alderson only.

Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though remaining in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving. The chair noted the committee now has open spots and encouraged members to reach out to citizens interested in participating.

actionItems: Minutes from previous meeting approved
April budget status report, TIFF detail reports, and legal details report acknowledged
CDA resolution needed this summer to call bonds for TIFF district payoff
Seek replacement committee member(s) for open positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, taking action on several ordinance amendments and addressing concerns about ebikes, parking regulations, and property issues. Key decisions included recommending approval of nuisance ordinance amendments, an ebike ordinance with emoto restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspections, address corrections, and a raze order for an abandoned structure.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Overnight parking regulations October 31st to May 1st
Request for crosswalk on Alderson Street
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Jim Senalt addressed the committee with concerns about the new farmers market location, predicting grass damage from vehicle parking and questioning ADA compliance for handicapped parking on grass. He also questioned the need for new fire inspections on food trucks and criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, suggesting the committee should receive regular updates on long-standing violations.

Code Enforcement reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and new owner submitted repair permits. Some enforcement-suspended cases may be reactivated for construction season. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with new officers starting in various months. The committee acknowledged reports 5, 6, and 7 on motion by Chwanka, seconded by Olsen, passed unanimously.

The committee discussed final amendments including tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet) over streets and walkways that were switched from the previous meeting. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees. Passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion on the model ebike ordinance for Chapter 82. Chief Hunt expressed strong opposition to emotos (electric dirt bikes) on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion included whether electric scooters used by middle schoolers should be affected. The committee decided to add language prohibiting emotos from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka to recommend approval with the emoto prohibition added. Passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction window (4am-4pm) is far outside what neighboring municipalities use (typically 4-5 hours overnight). Discussion included whether to eliminate overnight parking restrictions entirely or align with other communities. DPW Director Michael noted most snow plowing doesn't reach residential streets until 7-8am. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions for enforcement purposes. No action taken; staff will develop specific exception areas for discussion at the July meeting.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their facility and DC Everest parking lot where parishioners park. Committee discussed costs ($2,000-3,000 for basic crosswalk, $25,000-30,000 for lighted system). Chief Hunt reported no incidents at the location. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the cost, the committee recommends board approval. Passed unanimously. DPW Director will contact the church.

Fire Chief explained a regional consortium (Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston) will share food truck fire inspections under NFPA 54. One inspection with sticker is valid for all participating municipalities. Wausau opted out to do their own. Chief noted a recent inspection failed due to improper LP piping (galvanized steel) and improperly secured propane containers. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey to approve Ordinance 26-8. Passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (unknown duplex needing second address), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue for a new duplex on private drive. Chief Hunt mentioned an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments where each of 8 units has separate addresses. Motion made and seconded to recommend approval. Passed unanimously.

An abandoned structure since 2010-2011 that was too deteriorated for fire department training burn. Owners have been housing chickens inside. Current owners are up to date on taxes and prefer the village proceed with demolition, with costs added to tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey to authorize staff to begin the raze order. Passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment, criticizing the new farmers market location as 'terrible execution' that will destroy grassy areas, questioning ADA compliance for handicapped parking on grass, questioning the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks, and expressing concern that some code enforcement cases have been pending for years without visible progress.
actionItems: Recommend approval of Chapter 50 Nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees
Recommend approval of ebike ordinance with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property to Board of Trustees
DPW Director to develop list of specific areas requiring even/odd parking restrictions for July meeting
DPW Director Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for Alderson Street crosswalk
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Approve address corrections for Delicoski Street, Ross Avenue, and Radkey Avenue properties
Authorize staff to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property condition complaint at Sternberg and South Timber
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

agenda.item: Consideration of the minutes of the preceding meeting(s) - April 8, 2026 Regular Wausau Water Works Commission Minutes
Director's Reports - Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
Director's Reports - Wastewater Project Updates: Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station and Lift Station Assessment w/ Clark Dietz
Approving the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects (CIP) Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Approving the Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control (CCC) Program
Approving the Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners
Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

actionItems: Implement 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Execute sole source renewal agreement with HydroCorp for Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program
Proceed with sole source procurement of MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers for wastewater treatment
Next meeting scheduled for June 2, 2026 at 11:00 AM

title: Economic Development Committee Meeting
overview: The Economic Development Committee held its organizational meeting for the 2026-2028 term, with elections for chairperson and vice chairperson and establishment of a regular meeting schedule. The committee also considered deed restriction changes for 725 S. 84th Avenue and received an overview of the Community Development Department.

agenda.item: Elect Chairperson and Vice Chairperson for the 2026-2028 term
Establish regular meeting date and time for the 2026-2028 term
Consideration of April 6, 2026 Regular Economic Development Committee Minutes
Approving Consent to Transfer, Waiver of Right of First Refusal, Termination of Deed Restrictions and Approval of New Deed Restrictions for 725 S. 84th Avenue

discussions.item: Elect Chairperson and Vice Chairperson for the 2026-2028 term
discussions.body: The committee held elections for leadership positions for the new 2026-2028 term. Specific vote counts and election results are not recorded in the official vote records.

discussions.item: Establish regular meeting date and time for the 2026-2028 term
discussions.body: The committee established its regular meeting schedule for the 2026-2028 term. The specific date and time selected and any vote count are not recorded in the official vote records.
The committee considered the minutes from the April 6, 2026 Regular Economic Development Committee meeting. The outcome of any approval vote is not recorded in the official vote records.

discussions.item: Approving Consent to Transfer, Waiver of Right of First Refusal, Termination of Deed Restrictions and Approval of New Deed Restrictions for 725 S. 84th Avenue
discussions.body: The committee considered approving consent to transfer, waiver of right of first refusal, termination of existing deed restrictions, and approval of new deed restrictions for the property at 725 S. 84th Avenue. The vote outcome is not recorded in the official vote records.
The committee received a discussion item providing an overview of the Community Development Department. This was an informational item with no action required.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this special meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session to hold a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately and potentially take action regarding the student expulsion matter, with the possibility of reconvening in open session for further action if necessary.

agenda.item: Closed Session - Pupil Expulsion Hearing pursuant to s. 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), and s. 118.125 of Wisconsin Statutes

discussions.body: The Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session under Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), as well as section 118.125, to conduct a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately at the conclusion of the hearing and potentially take action in closed session if necessary and appropriate. Following the closed session, the board was scheduled to potentially reconvene into open session for further action.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to consider action on a pupil expulsion matter during or following the closed session hearing

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
actionItems: Bike Rack Request Form approved for implementation by Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee
Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
